role: Prosecutor General of the Islamic Revolution (1981–1985); Secretary-General, Assembly of Qom Seminary Scholars
bio:
Ayatollah Seyed Hossein Mousavi Tabrizi — born Seyed Hossein Pourmirghaffari — was an Iranian cleric who served as the second Prosecutor General of the Islamic Revolution and later reinvented himself as a reformist figure. He died on 12 April 2026 in Qom at the age of 79.

Born in 1946 in the Shotorban (Davachi) neighborhood of Tabriz, he began his seminary education in Tabriz before moving to Qom in 1962 and then to Najaf in 1966, where he studied under Grand Ayatollah Abu al-Qasim al-Khoei. He became a follower of [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) during the exile years and participated in the [Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979).

After the revolution, Mousavi Tabrizi was appointed as a Sharia judge and head of the [Islamic Revolutionary Courts](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-courts) in East and West Azerbaijan provinces, where he oversaw summary trials and mass executions. Following the suppression of Ayatollah [Kazem Shariatmadari](/people/kazem-shariatmadari)'s Muslim People's Republican Party in 1979–1980, he presided over a crackdown in Tabriz that resulted in at least 31 documented executions, with the actual toll believed to be higher. Trials routinely lasted only minutes, and defendants were denied legal counsel.

In September 1981, following the assassination of his predecessor [Ali Qoddousi](/people/ali-qoddousi), Mousavi Tabrizi was appointed Prosecutor General of the Islamic Revolution by Khomeini, becoming the second person to hold the post. In this role he publicly declared that opponents of the system 'must be executed' and that 'prisoners must be killed.' He oversaw the revolutionary prosecution apparatus during the most intense period of political repression in the early 1980s, working under Chief Justice [Abdolkarim Mousavi-Ardabili](/people/abdolkarim-mousavi-ardabili). In June 1983, he ordered the execution of 59 individuals in Tabriz, reportedly including minors. He also presided over the [Cinema Rex fire](/events/cinema-rex-fire-1978) case, sentencing Hossein Takbalizadeh and others to death in proceedings where, according to investigators, the actual perpetrators were never summoned. He was removed from the prosecutor post in 1985, after which the position was abolished.

Allegations of severe abuse of power during his tenure include coercing female prisoners into temporary marriages (sigheh) as a condition for judicial consideration, a practice documented by multiple sources.

He was elected to the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts) and later became Secretary-General of the [Assembly of Qom Seminary Scholars and Researchers](/orgs/assembly-of-qom-seminary-scholars), a reformist-leaning clerical body. He served as rotating president of the [Council for Coordinating the Reforms Front](/orgs/council-for-coordinating-reforms-front) (2015–2016) and as vice-president of the Pervasive Coalition of Reformists. In this later reformist phase, he questioned the state's treatment of youth, criticized arrest procedures following the [Mahsa Amini protests](/events/mahsa-amini-protests-2022), and advocated for prisoners' rights — a position that drew sharp criticism given his own role in the mass imprisonment and execution of political prisoners in the 1980s.

[Hossein-Ali Montazeri](/people/hossein-ali-montazeri), Khomeini's former designated successor, was among those who documented and criticized the judicial excesses of the revolutionary courts during the period of Mousavi Tabrizi's tenure. Mousavi Tabrizi's trajectory — from architect of mass executions to self-styled reformist — epitomizes the phenomenon of regime insiders who evade accountability for past atrocities while positioning themselves as advocates of change.
editorial_notes: Died 12 April 2026 in Qom. Born as Pourmirghaffari, adopted the name Mousavi Tabrizi. Listed on Iran Tribunal's 'List of Perpetrators'. His death prompted renewed public debate about accountability for 1980s judicial killings. The 1988-massacre tag reflects his role as former Prosecutor General during the apparatus that carried out the killings, though he had left the post by 1988 — he claimed MP status at the time but later defended the executions as justified.

sources.title: Hossein Mousavi Tabrizi: From Engineering Massacres to Wearing the Reformist Mantle
Death of a 1980s Prosecutor Revives Dark Legacy of Iran Regime's Judicial Repression
